coredata.dc:title: Utilising the interface interaction on tris(hydroxymethy)aminomethane-capped carbon dots to enhance the sensitivity and selectivity towards the detection of Co(II) ions
coredata.dc:creator: Ng, Yann Huey
Chin, Suk Fun
Pang, Suh Cem
Ng, Sing Muk
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a facile and green thermal carbonisation approach was adopted for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from α-cellulose. The conditions during the synthesis have been optimised to produce CDs with highest fluorescence intensity. Later, tris(hydroxymethy)aminomethane (TRIS) was introduced to the isolated CDs as capping agent on the interface of the CDs. TRIS was chosen due to the well reported capability to interact with various metal ions to form complexes. Particularly in this study, TRIS has enhanced the specificity and sensitivity features of CDs to detect cobalt (II) ions, (Co(II) ions). The UV–vis absorption spectroscopy study performed has found that the CDs can effectively interact with TRIS, while the assembled CDs/TRIS system later can form complex with Co(II) ions. In this case, TRIS has acted as the bridge to bring CDs and Co(II) ions to a close proximity in distance, which subsequently promoted the energy transfer due to the good overlapping in the emission of the CDs with the absorbance of the Co(II) ions. The process was observed clearly by the concentration dependent fluorescence quenching in the presence of increasing amount of Co(II) ions. This has been demonstrated for the first time and the system has been suggested to be used as a single shot detection probe for Co(II) ions in aqueous media. The probe was found to be effective for the detection of Co(II) ions with a linear response up to 1.87 mM and a limit of detection of 16.84 μM. This study has presented some new insights on the surface interface interaction between CDs and its capping agent and demonstrated its potential application for metal ions sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Capping
Sensing
Metal ions
Fluorescence
Quenching
Interface interaction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Physical appearance of crude carbonised products
Yield of CDs from different synthesis temperature
CDs250/TRIS interface interaction study with Co(II) ions

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Grant Scheme (FRGS) provided by Ministry of Education Malaysia (Grant No. FRGS/2/2013/SG06/SWIN/03/1). The authors would also like to acknowledge the supports from staffs, technicians and postgraduates of Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus and Universiti Malaysia Sarawak (UNIMAS) who have contributed to this study.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photographs of (a) untreated α-cellulose and the products after thermal carbonisation at different temperatures of (b) 200, (c) 225, (d) 250, (e) 275, (f) 300, (g) 350 and (h) 400 °C.
Fluorescence emission of CDs carbonised at different temperatures monitored at 435 nm upon excitation at 346 nm. Inset: Emission spectra of CDs synthesised at different temperatures.
The fluorescence emission recorded at 435 nm for CDs synthesised from α-cellulose at 250 °C in a furnace after different period of carbonisation.

The (a) absorbance, (b) excitation and (c) emission spectrum of CDs250 synthesised from α-cellulose via thermal carbonisation. Inset: Photograph of ultrapure water (left) and CDs250 (right) under exposure of UV light.
(A) TEM image of CDs250 and (B) size distribution of CDs250 in diameter based on TEM analysis.
FTIR absorption spectra for (a) untreated α-cellulose and (b) CDs250 carbonised in furnace at 250 °C for 30 min.

(A) Absorption spectra of CDs250 in the presence of different concentrations of TRIS (insert shows the absorbance spectrum for pure TRIS), and (B) showing the fluorescence spectra of CDs250 and CDs250 capped with TRIS (CDs250/TRIS).
Absorption spectra of CDs250/TRIS in the presence of different concentration ratios of Co(II) ions. Inset: Absorption spectra of Co(II) ions at different concentrations.
Absorption spectra of CDs250/TRIS in the presence of different concentration ratios of Ni(II) ions. Inset: Absorption spectra of different concentrations of Ni(II) ions.

The quenching of fluorescence spectrum for CDs250 without capping with TRIS in the presence of different concentration of Co(II) and Ni(II) ions (highest concentration tested for both metal ions was 2.3 × 10−3 M).

(A) The quenching of fluorescence spectrum for CDs250/TRIS in the presence of different concentration of Co(II) and Ni(II) ions, and (B)Stern-Volmer plots for the quenching trend for CDs250/TRIS in the presence of Co(II) and Ni(II) ions within the range of 0–1.87 mM using ultrapure water as baseline.
Various fluorescence intensity ratio (Fo − Fx)/Fo of the solutions of CDs250 and CDs250/TRIS in the presence of different metal ions at concentration of 4.55 mM.

head.ce:title: Utilising the interface interaction on tris(hydroxymethy)aminomethane-capped carbon dots to enhance the sensitivity and selectivity towards the detection of Co(II) ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Engineering, Computing and Science, Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus, Jalan Simpang Tiga, 93350 Kuching, Sarawak, Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Swinburne Sarawak Research Centre for Sustainable Technologies, Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus, Jalan Simpang Tiga, 93350 Kuching, Sarawak, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Swinburne Sarawak Research Centre for Sustainable Technologies
Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Resource Science and Technology, Universiti Malaysia Sarawak, 94300 Kota Samarahan, Sarawak, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Resource Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Faculty of Engineering, Computing and Science, Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus, Jalan Simpang Tiga, 93350 Kuching, Sarawak, Malaysia.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Computing and Science, Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a facile and green thermal carbonisation approach was adopted for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from α-cellulose. The conditions during the synthesis have been optimised to produce CDs with highest fluorescence intensity. Later, tris(hydroxymethy)aminomethane (TRIS) was introduced to the isolated CDs as capping agent on the interface of the CDs. TRIS was chosen due to the well reported capability to interact with various metal ions to form complexes. Particularly in this study, TRIS has enhanced the specificity and sensitivity features of CDs to detect cobalt (II) ions, (Co(II) ions). The UV–vis absorption spectroscopy study performed has found that the CDs can effectively interact with TRIS, while the assembled CDs/TRIS system later can form complex with Co(II) ions. In this case, TRIS has acted as the bridge to bring CDs and Co(II) ions to a close proximity in distance, which subsequently promoted the energy transfer due to the good overlapping in the emission of the CDs with the absorbance of the Co(II) ions. The process was observed clearly by the concentration dependent fluorescence quenching in the presence of increasing amount of Co(II) ions. This has been demonstrated for the first time and the system has been suggested to be used as a single shot detection probe for Co(II) ions in aqueous media. The probe was found to be effective for the detection of Co(II) ions with a linear response up to 1.87 mM and a limit of detection of 16.84 μM. This study has presented some new insights on the surface interface interaction between CDs and its capping agent and demonstrated its potential application for metal ions sensing.

ce:section.ce:para: The nano-sized fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) possess many unique physio-chemical properties and have recently captivated immense attention from various research areas on their fundamental science and their potential for real applications. CDs are relatively non-toxic, highly biocompatible and having tunable fluorescence property [1,2]. Subsequent post-synthesis steps such as surface passivation and the hybrid into nanocomposites can further enhance and/or modify the optical, chemical, and functional properties of the crude CDs [3,4]. Various compounds ranging from low molecular weight molecules with specific chemical functional groups to larger biomolecules that possess bioactivities can be employed as effective capping agents. Some examples of these capping agents are such as β-cyclodextrin and calix[4]arene derivatives [5], ethylenediamine [6], aptamer [7], and molecularly imprinted polymers [8]. In view of optical sensing application, it provides the opportunity to design the surface of the CDs with intended sensing receptors to enhance the specific interaction with a targeted analyte. The effective interface interaction will bring the analyte close to the CDs and potentially can induce energy transfer mechanisms in cases where the emission energy of the CDs is matching with the absorption energy of the analytes. Once this occurs, the initial optical property will be affected and followed by a change either in the intensity and/or band shift in wavelength of the emission from the CDs. Correlation can be established on the observed change of the optical signal with the concentration of the analyte. Eventually, this system can be utilised as a quantifying probe for a targeted analyte.

Based on the aforementioned key merits, the motivation of this study is to design an optical sensing probe using CDs that were surface functionalised with specific sensing receptors. The surface interaction of the capped CDs will be utilise to generate quantifiable signals to detect of specific analyte of interest. The targeted analyte chosen for this study is cobalt (II) ions, (Co(II) ions). Co(II) ions is an essential micronutrient in Vitamin B12 (hydroxocobalamin) that is required in trace amount for red blood cells (RBCs) production and prevention of anaemia [9]. However, exposure to excessive amount of cobalt especially Co(II) ions is associated with adverse health effects such as cardiomyopathy, hearing or visual impairment as well as diseases on haematological, thyroid, cardiovascular systems or even death [10,11]. Therefore, developing a rapid and sensitive tool for monitoring and detection of cobalt (Co(II)) ions is highly desired.

Tris(hydroxymethyl)-aminomethane (TRIS) that is also sometimes known as the 2-amino-2-(hydroxymethyl)-1,3-propanediol or THAM has been selected as the capping agent in this work and will be introduced to the surface of the CDs via physical absorption approach. TRIS is non-toxic in nature and commonly used as pH buffers for biochemical studies. In addition, the selection of TRIS was also driven by their unique chemical property, where it comprises of an amino group that is sterically hindered by three hydroxyl methyl groups attached to a tertiary carbon atom [12]. This feature allows TRIS to act as ligand to form complexes with certain metal ions through the amino and hydroxyl groups that are rich in electron density [13,14]. Besides, the additional hydroxyl groups can form strong hydrogen bonding with the surface of CDs that is normally rich with hydroxyl and carbonyl groups. Indirectly, TRIS will be the bridge to link the analyte near to the CDs, which the close distance often allows various energy transfer mechanisms to occur between the two species. One of the most common types is the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), which involves a donor fluorophore in an excited electronic state that is able to transfer its excitation energy to a nearby acceptor chromophore in a non-radiative route through long-range dipole–dipole interactions. Thus, quenching of the fluorophore will be observed. In this case, the donor fluorophore will be the CDs while the acceptor chromophore will be the Co(II) ions as analyte. It is also good that the TRIS is optically inert within the visible range studied and will not interfere with the sensing mechanism that happens to be in this range.

To our best knowledge, the development of CDs capped with TRIS on the surface that later being used as a sensing probe for the detection of Co(II) ions has not yet been reported. This study covers the synthesis of CDs and its optimisation works, capping study using TRIS, spectroscopy study on the complex formation, and the analytical merit evaluation of the CDs capped TRIS system for detecting Co(II) ions. This work also includes several comparison studies to demonstrate the significant of the surface functionalisation in promoting the sensitivity and selectivity of the CDs towards sensing Co(II) ions.

α-Cellulose purchased from Sigma-Aldrich was used as the carbon source for CDs synthesis. Tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (TRIS) was purchased from 1st Base Chemical, Malaysia. High purity metal salts of cadmium chloride (CdCl2), copper nitrate Cu(NO3)2, lead nitrate Pb(NO3)2, nickel nitrate Ni(NO3)2, cobalt nitrate Co(NO3)2 and zinc nitrate Zn(NO3)2 were purchased from Acros Organics, Malaysia. Mercuric chloride (HgCl2) was purchased from Fisher Chemical, Malaysia. All chemicals used were of at least of analytical grade and used as received without further purification unless otherwise stated. Ultrapure water obtained from Milipore Mili-Q Advantage-A10 and Milipore Elix-5 water purification system (∼18.2 MΩ cm, 25° C) was used throughout the study.

All fluorescence measurements were obtained using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse, Varian). The samples were diluted with appropriate amount of ultrapure water and transferred to a four-side clear quartz cuvette with a path length of 1.0 cm for fluorescence measurement. Excitation and emission slits were both set at 10 nm throughout the study. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Varian Cary 50). The absorbance measurements were recorded within the range of 200 nm to 800 nm. Ultrapure water was used as reference blank for the absorbance measurement. Isolated CDs samples were characterised by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL Model JEM 1010) and Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) (PerkinElmer Nicolet iS10).

CDs were prepared by thermal carbonisation of α-cellulose in a laboratory furnace (Carbolite ELF 11/14 B) adopting the general method reported previously with slight modification [15]. In a typical synthesis, accurately 1.0 g of α-cellulose was weighed into a crucible and carbonised in furnace at a fixed temperature for 30 min. Temperature used for the synthesis was optimised by carbonising α-cellulose at different temperatures ranging from 200 to 400 ° C. After carbonisation process, the resulting product was left cool to room temperature before suspended in 20 ml ultrapure water. The suspension was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min to remove bulk particles and the supernatant was filtered through 0.22 μm syringe filter. The CDs solution was then lyophilised for 18 h in freeze dryer (Labconco FreeZone 6) and kept at 4 °C until used. The isolated CDs was then surface functionalised with TRIS by mixing 1.0 ml of CDs solution to 1.0 ml 0.5 M TRIS solution, vortex to mix well and left to stir for 10 min at room condition to facilitate chelating balance.

Stock solution of 0.1 M Co(II) and Ni(II) ions were prepared from the respective salts to be tested with TRIS-functionalised CDs. The fluorescence signal was monitored at 435 nm upon excitation at 346 nm. The full absorption spectra ranging from 200 to 800 nm was recorded. Both fluorescence and absorbance were measured simultaneously and the results were later used to determine the surface interface interaction between the CDs and metal ions. For interference study, stock solutions of various metal ions of cadmium(II) (Cd(II)), copper(II) (Cu(II)), mercury(II) (Hg(II)), lead(II) (Pb(II)) and zinc(II) (Zn(II)) ions with concentration of 0.1 M were prepared from the respective metal salts. The functionalised CDs solution was then tested against the prepared metal ions solutions to identify any potential interfering metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical appearance of crude carbonised products
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, a facile one-step thermal carbonisation approach has been adopted to synthesis the CDs from α-cellulose using a furnace under atmospheric pressure and composition. α-Cellulose is the insoluble fibrous residue that can be commonly found in agricultural wastes. Thus, this makes them a good alternative as starting precursor since they are readily available, cheap and classified as green and renewable resources. This also promotes CDs to be a more sustainable alternative of fluorophores for sensing applications [16]. In order to observe the carbonisation process, the α-cellulose was treated at different temperatures in a furnace for 30 min and the resulting products were photographed as shown in Fig. 1 .

Cleavage of glycosidic linkages of α-cellulose would occur during the thermal carbonisation treatment and subsequently reducing the length of the polymer chains. This resulted in the production of carbon dioxide, water and a variety of hydrocarbon derivatives [17]. Fluorescent CDs was likely to be one of the products formed during this transition stage, where the reactive intermediate chemical species could re-polymerise to form the nanostructures consisting of mainly aromatic hydrocarbons. The CDs formed can possibly inherit some chemical moieties from the initial starting precursor due to incomplete carbonisation. A clear colour gradient could be observed from the end-products obtained after being thermally treated at different temperatures. The photographs clearly show that α-cellulose as the starting precursor before carbonisation was of fine white powder. There was no significant change in colour when the α-cellulose was heated at 200 °C for 30 min, indicating that the carbonisation is not effective when performed below this threshold temperature. However, a light yellowish product had started to yield when the starting precursor was heated at 225 °C for the same duration. Once the carbonisation temperature was increased to 250 °C, it was observed that the fine white α-cellulose powder had turned into light brown-coloured product. At elevated temperature of 275 °C, dark brown product was produced and darker residues were isolated when α-cellulose was treated at temperature beyond 275 °C. The black fine powder was most probably the chars produced as a result of complete carbonisation of α-cellulose. Formation of char due to decomposition of α-cellulose was suggested to involve the breakage of 1,4-glucosidic bonds leading to depolymerisation. Moreover, ring opening of the α-cellulose structure could be induced by breaking of 1,5-acetal bonds during carbonisation [18]. This study has concluded that the carbonisation can be performed using thermal treatment at atmospheric condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Yield of CDs from different synthesis temperature
ce:section.ce:para: After separate batches of α-cellulose were treated at different temperatures in the furnace, the resulting product from each batch was added with ultrapure water before being centrifuged and filtered to remove those insoluble large residues. Eventually, the CDs will be isolated in the ultrapure water since their tiny size will enable them to remain as colloidal suspension and dispersed homogeneously throughout the solvent. This samples isolated were profiled for their fluorescence property and the maximum emission under the optimum excitation has been recorded for each batch as shown in Fig. 2 . Since only CDs were showing fluorescence property and observed with not much peak shift from the different batches, the emission intensity has been generally taken as the semi-quantitative indicator for the amount of CDs present in each sample. Low intensity indicates small amount and vice versa.

α-Cellulose is fine white fibrous material that is insoluble in water and possess no or low fluorescence property when viewed under ultraviolet light. Moreover, no signal could be detected when a suspension of α-cellulose was measured using a fluorescence spectrophotomete. Similarly, the CDs isolated from the sample carbonised at 200 °C displayed only slight and insignificant fluorescence. The thermal energy provided at this temperature was suggested to be insufficient to activate the depolymerisation process, which then would not allow the formation of hydrocarbon intermediates that lead to the production of CDs. The fluorescence intensity increased with increasing carbonisation temperature as more complete decomposition could be achieved at higher activation energy. The result showed that CDs with the highest fluorescence intensity could be obtained when α-cellulose was carbonised at 250 °C for 30 min, thus was chosen to be the optimum temperature for the synthesis.

The result shows clearly that the fluorescence intensity for the batches obtained at temperature higher than 250 °C had started to decrease gradually. At the temperature of 400 °C, significantly low with almost no fluorescence intensity could be detected. Elevated temperatures have eventually led to the excessive and complete combustion, which have severely decomposed the hydrocarbon intermediates into chars, tars and gaseous products that will not exhibit fluorescence property [19].

The inset of Fig. 2 shows the full emission spectrum of CDs prepared at different temperatures when being excited at 346 nm. All CDs samples obtained at different temperatures showed maximum fluorescence emission centred at around 435 nm. There was no significant variation in wavelength of the fluorescence emission when different carbonisation temperatures were employed for decomposition of α-cellulose. This in turn strongly suggested that similar species of CDs was present even the carbonisation condition was varied. The FWHM of the fluorescence emission bands of the CDs obtained at different temperatures were all evaluated to be less than 100 nm which were comparable to the value reported in literature [20]. FWHM is often used to indicate the homogeneity in the quality of the CDs, especially in terms of the morphology and origin of the fluorescence.

The optimum duration for the thermal carbonisation reaction of α-cellulose to form CDs was also investigated. The carbonisation temperature was fixed at 250 °C since this temperature could initiate carbonisation to produce CDs with highest fluorescence intensity compared to the other temperatures tested in the previous section. The carbonisation duration was varied for different batches and the subsequent products isolated after the carbonisation were isolated using standard procedure. In this case, the sample isolated was excited at 346 nm and the emission wavelength was monitored at 435 nm, similar to the procedure carried out for the optimisation of carbonisation temperature in the previous section. The peak emissions recorded for each batch are as shown in Fig. 3 .

As mentioned before, the process of CDs formation would first involve bond cleavage of α-cellulose into active hydrocarbon intermediates due to thermal decomposition, which later will re-polymerise or grow into nanoparticles of CDs during the transition of the reaction. Thus, sufficient time will be required to complete the process in order to obtain the highest yield of CDs. Short synthesis period might not be able to complete the carbonisation process of the given precursor and will produce less CDs. Similarly, once the process has completed and there is no more leftover of starting precursor in the system, the yield will also no longer increase even with extended synthesis duration was given as shown by the result. Conversely, slight drop of intensity was observed for prolonged synthesis in the furnace. This trend was also observed in one of the previous works, where it was suggested to be caused by decomposition of the freshly formed CDs under continuous heating [21]. Thus, prolonged synthesis time after complete carbonisation will only lead to the wastage of cost, energy and time. In this case, the carbonisation time of 30 min was found to be sufficient to complete the synthesis of CDs using 1.0 g of α-cellulose under the carbonisation temperature of 250 °C.

The CDs for the following studies in this work were synthesised under the optimum conditions identified in the earlier sections. Isolation of the CDs was performed using the extraction by ultrapure water as the medium. However, those bulk carbonised residues were filtered using a syringe filter of 0.22 μm and by conventional centrifugation process. The CDs were in the nano-sized format and this dimension can usually dispersible well in water to form colloidal suspension. Since the CDs were produced from carbonisation in the presence of atmospheric oxygen, oxidation could have occurred simultaneously during the harsh carbonisation process, leading to the formation of carbonyl and hydroxyl rich groups of the surface of the CDs. The presence of these groups usually would promote better solubility and dispersibility of the CDs in water. Larger particles such as those of more than 100 nm were removed after centrifugation. The finally isolated CDs was thereafter depicted as CDs250, referring to optimum synthesis temperature and was kept at 4 °C under dark container until further use. The yield from 1.0 g of α-cellulose was determined to produce 28 mg of CDs250 which was about 2.8% w/w% of the mass of the starting material.

It is crucial to identified that the fluorescence recorded is indeed from the CDs isolated after the carbonisation of α-cellulose. One of the approach taken was viewing the morphology for the product isolated under the TEM. In this case, the sample was first dispersed in ultrapure water followed by dripping it onto formvar-coated copper grids. The droplet was left to dry and viewed under a TEM. The image showed the sample consisted with nanoparticles and quite clean form other species as presented in Fig. 5 A. Based on the image, we have confirmed that sample solution was containing the CDs250, which showed to have homogeneous morphology with the shape that was mostly quasispherical. The size distribution of the CDs250 is focused around 40–60 nm in diameter with average sizes of approximately 50 nm (Fig. 5B). Besides, the CDs250 were not in clusters format, while quite well dispersed into individual nanoparticles. This indicates that the surface could have chemical functional groups of similar property that repel each other. It is also worth noting that traces of fibrous residues could be observed from the TEM image. This observation has suggested the presence of fibrous α-cellulose residues in trace amount that could be due to the incomplete carbonisation of the starting precursor.

The organic functional groups on the surface of CDs250 were identified using FTIR. The FTIR spectra of the pure un-carbonised α-cellulose and CDs250 isolated after carbonisation at 250 °C are presented in Fig. 6 . The FTIR spectrum of CDs250 showed OH stretch in the alcohol or phenol group at 3334.94 cm−1, alkane CH stretch at 2915.89 cm−1, aromatic CC and CC stretch at 1602.84 and 1411.69 cm−1 respectively, carboxylic acid CO stretch at 1000–1320 cm−1, COC in pyranose ring at 1038.71 cm−1 and aromatic CH stretch at 895.78 cm−1. The presence of trace amount of fibrous residues in the CDs250 sample as observed previously TEM study may resulted in causing the similar profile for the spectral generated from CDs compared to α-cellulose. However, it was observed that vibration stretch of carboxylic acid at 1725.53 cm−1 that can be found in CDs250 was not present in the spectrum of the untreated α-cellulose. It was also observed that the amide CO stretch at 1641 cm−1 significantly increased after thermal carbonisation of α-cellulose. All these have suggested that the oxidation process has occurred due to the treatment of high temperature in the presence of environmental oxygen (approx. 20%), which led to the formation of more carbonyl groups on the surface of the CDs250. The FTIR profile indicates that functional groups such as alcohol or phenol, alkane, aromatic groups and carboxylic acid were present and forming the surface defects of CDs250. The functional groups will later play a crucial role in anchoring the capping agent during surface modification of the CDs250. In this work, the sample of CDs capped with TRIS that was prepared at later part of this work was not studied using FTIR. This is because the interaction nature was of non-covalent type and in the equilibrium under aqueous environment. It will be quite difficult to pick up the change in the FTIR peak on these weaker interaction points compared to those covalent bonds.

The CDs250 were surface functionalised with TRIS as capping agent with the theoretical motivation to increase the interaction with Co(II) ions, which is the analyte of interest for this study. Instead of FTIR, the UV–vis spectrophotometry was adopted to study the interface interaction of CDs250 and TRIS. TRIS was observed to show no obvious absorption band within 250–800 nm at the concentration range used for this study (Inset Fig. 7 A). However, it has caused the change in UV–vis spectra of CDs250 in ultrapure water that was dependent on the concentrations of TRIS (Fig. 7A). Bare CDs250 dissolved in ultrapure water exhibited an absorption band at 270 nm with a tail extending to the visible region as mentioned earlier. This absorption peak has showed a decrease trend with the presence of increasing amount of TRIS in the solution. An isosbestic point centred at 230 nm was eventually observed from the spectral recorded with the varying amount of TRIS added. Isosbestic point can be referred as a point to a set of absorption spectra where the sum of concentration for principal absorbing components is constant when plotted on a same graph [25]. Isosbestic point commonly appears in system that undergoes chemical reaction such as formation of product from reactant that is in progress or on a solution in which two absorbing components are in equilibrium [25].

A clear isosbestic point observed in this study has indicated the formation of complexes as a result of interaction between TRIS and CDs250. Hence, it was postulated that the CDs250 nanoparticles were successfully modified with TRIS via physical absorption forces, which the assembly was thereafter referred as CDs250/TRIS. The interaction is suggested to be due to non-covalent link, but more towards physical absorption of TRIS on CDs250 via intermolecular forces such as hydrogen bonding and Van der Waals forces. This can happen since the CDs250 were rich in functional groups such as OH and COOH, while the TRIS has OH and NH3 groups that would induce hydrogen bonding. Such paring was similar to the work reported by Liu et al. [26], which have confirmed that the interaction between the CDs and the folic acid in their study was mainly due to hydrogen bonding and Van der Waals forces. In term of the fluorescence spectrum, the addition of TRIS has caused a slight enhancement of the intensity without drastic shift in the wavelength of the emission peak (Fig. 7B). This further support the postulate that the TRIS was capped on the surface of CDs250 and can shield the CDs250 from the surrounding quenchers such as solvent molecules and traces of contaminants.

Due to the presence of amine and hydroxyl groups that are rich in electrons, TRIS has the ability to form complexes with metal ions via coordination bonding. Previous report has showed that TRIS can form complexes with various metal ions with different affinities [27]. They showed 1:1 (TRIS: Metal) ratio complex in most cases, while also possible to increase the ratio to 2:1 in the presence of high concentration of TRIS. Generally, TRIS has showed higher binding affinity with cobalt(II), nickel(II), copper(II), zinc(II), cadmium(II) and lead(II) ions compared to the other commonly available metal ions [27]. Thus, by having TRIS capped on the CDs250, it should increase the potential to interact with this group of cations and can possibly promote the quenching of the fluorescence from the CDs250 that can be used for sensing application. Since Co(II) ions is within this group, the use of TRIS has high potential to act as the sensing receptor when capped on the surface of the CDs250. In addition, Co(II) ions is the only metal ions that possesses a broad absorption band across 400–600 nm among the other mentioned cations. This property will increase the tendency of energy transfer from the CDs250/TRIS system that emitted at around 435 nm to the Co(II) ions. Spectral overlapping is one of the key requirements for the FRET to happen besides the need for a close proximity in the distance between the donor and acceptor [28]. The near distance can be achieved once the complex was formed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs250/TRIS interface interaction study with Co(II) ions
ce:section.ce:para: Spectroscopic techniques employing both UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy were used to study the interface interaction of the CDs250/TRIS system with Co(II) ions. In this study, Ni(II) ions was selected as a reference system for comparison purpose. Ni(II) ions was selected due to the similarity in the physiochemical properties, i.e. both Co(II) and Ni(II) ions are transition metals, classified in Group VIII of the periodic table, with atomic numbers of 27 and 28 respectively [29], and with similar ionic radius of 0.72 Å for Co(II) and 0.69 Å for Ni(II) ions [30]. Additionally, Co(II) and Ni(II) ions have the same binding site as Fe(II) ions in biological enzymes [30].

The absorption spectra of CDs250/TRIS in the presence of different concentrations of Co(II) and Ni(II) ions are as shown in Figs. 8 and 9
respectively. It clearly shows that the presence of Co(II) ions has significantly shifted the initial absorbance band of the CDs250/TRIS. This is a strong evidence of a complex formation, where the complex formed has created as new energy bandgap that caused the absorbance energy to shift. As the concentration ratio of CDs250/TRIS over Co(II) ions was increased, the initial absorbance peak at 510 nm for Co(II) was also observed to have decreased, whereas the absorbance below 440 nm started to increase as a result of the newly formed complex. Hence, the decrease of peak at 510 nm was due to the consumption of Co(II) ions to form the complex. The overlay of the spectra has also clearly indicated the formation of an isosbestic point, which is often used as an indication for the formation of complexes or products from the initial reactants [31,32]. The overall spectra pattern is different from the spectra of bare Co(II) ions at different concentrations in the absence of CDs250/TRIS (inset Fig. 8). Both intrinsic absorption peaks of bare Co(II) ions at 300 nm and 510 nm decreased as the concentration of Co(II) ions.

As for the CDs250/TRIS that was added with the Ni(II) ions, there was just a slight shift of the initial absorbance of CDs250/TRIS. As the concentration ratio of CDs250/TRIS over Ni(II) ions increases, no new formation of peak was observed but just recorded the decrease in the overall absorbance band. This could be postulated as due to the drop in the amount of Ni(II) ions in the system since absorbance band profile was exactly identical to those recorded from samples containing only Ni(II) ions (inset Fig. 9). As compared to those added with Co(II) ions, there was also no clear isosbestic point observed. This strongly suggests that the surface interaction of Ni(II) ions on the surface of CDs250/TRIS was less effective in altering the absorbance electronic bandgap of the system.

The effect on the presence of Co(II) ions towards the fluorescence property of the original CDs250/TRIS was evaluated for their potential as sensing system. Similar to the UV–vis absorption study, the effect due to the presence of Ni(II) ions was also studied for comparison purpose. In this case, the addition of Co(II) and Ni(II) ions directly into the solution containing the bare CDs250 without capping has showed very limited effect on the fluorescence property (Fig. 10 ). This indicates weak interaction of the CDs250 with the free metal ions in the solution and without near distance, it is difficult for the suggested FRET mechanism to occur effectively. However, when the CDs250 was capped with TRIS, the fluorescence intensity has decreased significantly in the presence of Co(II) ions as illustrated in the fluorescence spectrums and the Stern-Volmer plot (Fig. 11). Similarly, CDS250/TRIS system that was added with Ni(II) ions also showed enhancement in the degree of quenching but not as significant to those observed when added with Co(II) ions. This has supported the postulate of the study where the TRIS as capping agent has managed to cause effective interface interaction between the CDs250 and Co(II) ions and subsequently promoted the efficiency of the FRET mechanism. Capping has played a crucial role in enabling the quenching. The complex formation has allowed effective energy transfer to occur from the emission of the CDs250 to the absorption band of the Co(II) ions [33–35]

As mentioned, there was only slight quenching of CDs250/TRIS when added with Ni(II) ions. One possible reasons could be due to a weaker interaction as suggested by the absorbance study and thus less effective energy transfer is expected. TRIS is a potential metal-chelating ligand for certain metal ions [13,14] and possible that Ni(II) ions would actually interact with TRIS to form complexes such as [Ni(TRIS)]2+, [Ni(TRIS)2]2+ and [Ni(C3H4N2)]+ [36]. However, the reaction between Ni(II) ions and TRIS was reported to be extremely slow [36]. The reaction performed by ordinary titration may last several days, even then equilibrium may not be reached at all points [37]. Additionally, the Ni(II) ions has narrower absorbance peak at around 400 nm and this peak is only slightly overlapped with the emission of CDs250/TRIS at the shoulder of the band. Unlike Co(II) ions, the broad absorbance band has significantly overlapped with the emission of CDs250/TRIS and this will allow high efficiency of FRET to occur, leading to the significant fluorescence quenching.

The quenching caused by the Co(II) and Ni(II) ions was found to be concentration dependent, but no direct linear relationship was observed as shown in Fig. 11 A. Thus, the standard Stern-Volmer method was employed to analyse the fluorescence quenching trend and this has managed to linearize the relationship within the studied concentration range as shown in Fig. 11B. In this study, the addition of the metal ions was based on micro-titration approach and thus, ultrapure water was used as baseline to correct the effect of dilution as the volume of the system increased gradually during titration. However, it is worth noting that the dilution cause by the water had no significant effect on the initial fluorescence of CDs250/TRIS within the volume range adopted in this study.

As expected from the strong interface interaction, the Co(II) ions have the steepest slope for the Stern-Volmer plot compared to the Ni(II) ions and water. The Stern-Volmer constant was evaluated to be 2.53 mM−1. The linear analytical window was obtained up to the concentration of 1.87 mM and the limit of detection (LOD) was evaluated to be 16.84 μM using 3σ/s, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank signal of CDs250/TRIS (n = 6) and s is the slope of the plot. This detection range will be applicable to be used for the monitoring of Co(II) intakes from different sources such as food and drinking water, which are known to be the major exposure routes for the populations [11]. A pre-concentration process using suitable absorption materials can be performed for those applications that require lower detection limit [38]. Besides, it can be used as quick quantification probe for those industries that deal with Co(II) ions in high concentration range such as the textile and battery manufacturing factories. Comparatively, the slope obtained from the plot due to the addition of Ni(II) ions was far flatter compared to the one added with Co(II) ions, indicating ineffective energy transfer. The Stern-Volmer constant was evaluated to be just 0.13 mM−1, slightly above the slope value of 0.01 mM−1 that was obtained due to the decrease of intensity caused by the dilution factor. As such, the results have supported strongly that the developed CDs250/TRIS has great potential for Co(II) ions sensing.

The sensitivity and selectivity of CDs towards detecting Co(II) ion were enhanced by the presence of TRIS and this was strongly suggested as due to the formation of complex that has promoted the quenching. However, there are other types of metal ions that have been reported to interact with good affinity with TRIS as well, i.e. Co(II), Ni(II), Cu(II), Zn(II), Cd(II), Hg(II), and Pb(II) ions, which potentially can cause serious interference effect if they are to be behaving similarly as to the Co(II) ions. Thus, these few metal ions have been chosen particularly by this work to be used as candidates for the testing in the interference study. The degree of fluorescence intensity change in the presence of different metal ions was evaluated in terms of the ratio (Fo − Fx)/Fo, where Fo is the initial fluorescence intensity and Fx is the intensity recorded after the addition of the respective metal ions.

From the result obtained (Fig. 12 ), the most significant signal change was obtained from Co(II) ions where the enhancement in the degree of quenching was recorded in the presence of TRIS. In the absence of TRIS, the bare CDs250 showed slight increment of fluorescence instead of quenching when Co(II) ions was added. Similar trend was obtained for Cd(II) ions, but was at less significant increment. The enhancement of intensity is often postulated to be due to weak redox reaction that could possibly happen at the surface [39]. There is report showing that CDs could enhance electrocatalytic performance and allowed faster reduction process due to the presence of abundant oxygen species on their surface [40]. Hence, enhancing the surface states of bare CDs by further oxidation can promote better redox efficiently, which in turn leads to enhanced fluorescence.

As for the Cu(II), Hg(II) and Zn(II) ions, the fluorescence quenching on the bare CDs250 was more significant as compared with CDs250 capped with TRIS. This result suggests that these three species could interact more effectively with the oxidised surface of the bare CDs250, while presence of TRIS at the surface could have shielded this interaction thus retarded the quenching. Not all capping can promote better quenching [41]. It means that the capping in this case has promoted better selectivity for the sensing system toward Co(II) ions by shielding the interference from Cd(II) ions particularly. As for Ni(II) and Pb(II) ions, the TRIS was shown to promote better surface interaction as proposed and caused enhanced quenching effect as well. However, the degree of quenching was less effective compared to that of Co(II) ions. In addition, the interference effect can be reduced by first shielding these two species using ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) at a controlled pH condition. This is possible since the formation constants for Ni(II) and Pb(II) are higher compared to Co(II) ions [42]. Thus, by reducing the pH condition with an acid will eventually weaken the formation constants across all the EDTA-metal complexes. A point will be reached when the interaction of EDTA-Co(II) will be weakened and Co(II) ions will be released free, whereas the Ni(II) and Pb(II) ions will be still intact due to the stronger formation constants.

In summary, CDs obtained from simple thermal carbonisation of α-cellulose has been functionalised with TRIS and characterised using spectroscopic techniques. The synthesis of CDs in this work was easy, cost effective and involved no other reagents or solvents. It was also proven that capping with TRIS has enhanced the sensitivity and selectivity towards Co(II) ions, and the system has been evaluated to have an analytical window of 16.84 μM to 1.87 mM. This study has provided new insight on utilising the surface interface interaction on CDs via capping agent and demonstrated its promising application for chemical sensing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Grant Scheme (FRGS) provided by Ministry of Education Malaysia (Grant No. FRGS/2/2013/SG06/SWIN/03/1). The authors would also like to acknowledge the supports from staffs, technicians and postgraduates of Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus and Universiti Malaysia Sarawak (UNIMAS) who have contributed to this study.

Characterization and studies of some four, five and six coordinate transition and representative metal complexes of tris-(hydroxymethyl)-aminomethane

Sustainable alternative in environmental monitoring using carbon nanoparticles as optical probes

Analysis of different kinetic models in the dynamic pyrolysis of cellulose
Excitation-independent carbon dots, from photoluminescence mechanism to single-color application
Synthesis of carbon nanoparticles from waste rice husk used for the optical sensing of metal ions

Hydrothermal route for cutting graphene sheets into blue-luminescent graphene quantum dots

Implications of a Novel Interpretation of the Isosbestic Point

HIF-1, an oxygen and metal responsive transcription factor
Spectrophotometric determination of alendronate in pharmaceutical formulations via complex formation with Fe (III) ions
Use of isosbestic point wavelength shifts to estimate the fraction of a precursor that is converted to a given product
Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications
Photoinduced electron transfers with carbon dots

High efficiency platinum nanoparticles based on carbon quantum dot and its application for oxygen reduction reaction
Interface study on zinc oxide quantum dots using fluorometric and regression analysis in view of optical sensing

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yann Huey Ng has completed her MSc at the Faculty of Engineering, Computing and Science, Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus, Malaysia in 2017. She received her BSc in Biotechnology from Swinburne University of Technology in 2013. Her research interest and expertise are in the area of utilising novel nanoparticles for optical sensing applications.

Suk Fun Chin is an Associate Professor in Materials Chemistry at the Faculty of Resource Science and Technology, Universiti Malaysia Sarawak. She received her Ph.D. degree from University of Western Australia, Australia in 2009. Her current research interests focus on synthesis, characterisation and application of nanostructured materials for various applications such as controlled release, energy storage and biosensing.

Suh Cem Pang is currently a Professor of Chemistry at the Faculty of Resource Science and Technology, Universiti Malaysia Sarawak, Malaysia. He received his BSc (Hons), MSc, and PhD degrees from the University of Malaya, Malaysia, University of York, UK, and University of Wisconsin-Madison, USA in 1982, 1991, and 2000, respectively. His research interests are nanomaterials, biopolymers, nanoparticulate transition metal oxide thin films, biosensors and energy storage devices.

Sing Muk Ng is currently the Acting Director for the Swinburne Sarawak Research Centre of Sustainable Technologies and Associate Professor at the Faculty of Engineering, Computing and Science, Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus, Malaysia. He obtained his BSc and Ph.D. degrees in the areas of chemical engineering and analytical science from the Malaysia National University, Malaysia and University of Manchester, United Kingdom in 2004 and 2008 respectively. His current research interests include development of bio/chemical optical sensors and light instrumentations for chemical sensing applications.
